terms.definition: A collection of parts and sub-assemblies positioned using placement constraints in a .asm file. Assemblies define how components fit together in the final product.
An extrude depth option that extends the feature to a specified numeric distance from the sketch plane. Used when the exact depth dimension is known.
Construction geometry in the Sketcher used to define axes of symmetry and revolution. Centerlines are not part of the final sketch profile and do not appear on the 3D model.
A geometric rule applied to sketch entities that controls their shape and position. Examples include horizontal, vertical, perpendicular, tangent, equal, symmetric, and coincident.
A reference feature that defines the origin point and X, Y, Z axis directions. The default coordinate system (PRT_CSYS_DEF) is created automatically in every new part.
A reference line used for patterns, revolves, and measurements. Created at the intersection of two datum planes, through edges, or through the center of cylindrical features.

An infinite flat reference surface used as a sketch plane, feature placement reference, mirror plane, or measurement reference. Three default datum planes (RIGHT, TOP, FRONT) are created in every new part.
A reference point placed on surfaces, edges, vertices, or at specific coordinates. Used for measurements, coordinate system creation, and assembly constraints.
The automatic link between related Creo files. When a part dimension changes, every assembly and drawing referencing that part updates automatically, eliminating manual synchronization.

The functional purpose and behavior captured in a model through dimensions, constraints, and feature relationships. A well-captured design intent ensures the model behaves correctly when parameters change.
A numerical value that controls the size or position of sketch geometry or feature parameters. Types include linear, radial/diameter, angular, and reference dimensions.
A 2D engineering document (.drw file) generated from 3D parts or assemblies. Contains views, dimensions, annotations, and manufacturing information.
A feature that creates 3D geometry by projecting a 2D sketch profile along a direction perpendicular to the sketch plane. Can add or remove material with various depth options.
A discrete modeling operation that adds or removes material from the solid model. Features are the building blocks of Creo models and are listed in the Model Tree in creation order.
A modeling approach where 3D shapes are built by combining individual features (extrudes, revolves, rounds, etc.), each capturing a specific aspect of the design.
The central 3D viewport in Creo Parametric where the model is displayed and manipulated. Supports real-time rotation, zoom, and pan using the middle mouse button.
A display control that makes a feature invisible in the graphics area without affecting its geometry, mass properties, or child features. Used to reduce visual clutter.
A hierarchical panel listing all features, datums, and components in the current model in chronological creation order. Provides direct access to feature editing, reordering, and management.
The collapsible left-side panel in Creo Parametric containing the Model Tree, Layer Tree, and other navigation tools.
A modeling method where geometry is driven by parameters (dimensions and constraints) that capture design intent. Changing parameter values automatically regenerates the model.
A feature that provides references (surfaces, edges, datums) used by other features. Modifying a parent automatically updates its children. Deleting a parent may cause its children to fail.
A feature that depends on references from a parent feature. Child features automatically update when their parent changes. If the parent is deleted, child features may fail.
A 3D solid model (.prt file) containing features that define its shape. Parts are the basic building blocks and can be used independently or placed in assemblies.
The process of recalculating the model geometry after a change. Creo regenerates features from top to bottom in the Model Tree order, updating parent-child dependencies.
An extrude option that creates a cut or pocket by removing material from the existing solid. Used for slots, holes, grooves, and cutouts.
The context-sensitive toolbar at the top of the Creo Parametric window, organized into tabs (Model, Analysis, Annotate, Render, View) containing groups of related commands.
A 2D cross-section profile created in the Sketcher environment. Sketches define the shape of features like extrudes and revolves. Requires a sketch plane, orientation, and references.
A feature control that temporarily removes a feature from the model geometry, display, and mass calculations. Unlike Hide, suppress affects child features and improves regeneration performance.
An extrude depth option that extends the feature equally on both sides of the sketch plane. Ensures the feature is centered regardless of sketch plane position.
An extrude depth option that extends the feature through the entire existing model geometry. Commonly used for cuts that must pass completely through a part.
The default folder where Creo looks for and saves files. Should be set at the start of each session to ensure files are organized and accessible.
